issue_title: Command line arguments to jq are broken since 2.3.5
issue_body:
Passing any jq command line arguments after yq 2.3.4 are not longer correctly passed to jq and the user is presented with the ouput of `yq --help`.  For example,

```console
yq --yaml-output --from-file [jqfilter] [file]
usage: yq [options] <jq filter> [YAML file...]
          jq_filter [files [files ...]]

yq: Command-line YAML processor - jq wrapper for YAML documents

...
```
```console
yq --compact-output [file]
usage: yq [options] <jq filter> [YAML file...]
          jq_filter [files [files ...]]

yq: Command-line YAML processor - jq wrapper for YAML documents
...
```

issue_title: add conditional breakpoint UI support
issue_body:
Add an input to supply a condition for a breakpoint, and display the condition for the breakpoint if there is one.

Documentation copied below from https://www.sourceware.org/gdb/onlinedocs/gdb/GDB_002fMI-Breakpoint-Commands.html#GDB_002fMI-Breakpoint-Commands

> The -break-condition Command
Synopsis
 -break-condition number expr
Breakpoint number will stop the program only if the condition in expr is true. The condition becomes part of the ‘-break-list’ output (see the description of the ‘-break-list’ command below).

> GDB Command
The corresponding GDB command is ‘condition’.

> Example
(gdb)
-break-condition 1 1
^done
(gdb)
-break-list
^done,BreakpointTable={nr_rows="1",nr_cols="6",
hdr=[{width="3",alignment="-1",col_name="number",colhdr="Num"},
{width="14",alignment="-1",col_name="type",colhdr="Type"},
{width="4",alignment="-1",col_name="disp",colhdr="Disp"},
{width="3",alignment="-1",col_name="enabled",colhdr="Enb"},
{width="10",alignment="-1",col_name="addr",colhdr="Address"},
{width="40",alignment="2",col_name="what",colhdr="What"}],
body=[bkpt={number="1",type="breakpoint",disp="keep",enabled="y",
addr="0x000100d0",func="main",file="hello.c",fullname="/home/foo/hello.c",
line="5",cond="1",thread-groups=["i1"],times="0",ignore="3"}]}
(gdb)

issue_body: `gdb` has the ability to [save breakpoints](https://sourceware.org/gdb/current/onlinedocs/gdb/Save-Breakpoints.html#Save-Breakpoints) to a file as well as [load them](https://sourceware.org/gdb/current/onlinedocs/gdb/Command-Files.html#Command-Files) across sessions. issue_title: pylama doesn't detect too-few-format-args
issue_body:
When I run a native installation of pylint, its able to detect this error:

E: 75,21: Not enough arguments for format string (too-few-format-args)

On the following line of code:

               print "{}{}Tree-Type: {}".format(
                      prefix, prefix. TreeType.NON_STANDARD)

The corrected code is as follows:

               print "{}{}Tree-Type: {}".format(
                      prefix, prefix, TreeType.NON_STANDARD)

But when I run pylama, it doesn't detect the issue.

Here's what I'm running:

# pylama --version
pylama 7.4.3
# python --version
Python 2.7.5

issue_title: PubSub data corrupt?
issue_body:
I'm trying to port a gevent_pyzmq app over to Twisted, in which I'm doing something like this:

``` python
while True:
        message = receiver.recv()
        market_json = zlib.decompress(message)
```

The equivalent in my txZMQ module:

``` python
zf = ZmqFactory()
e = ZmqEndpoint('connect', 'tcp://master.eve-emdr.com:8050')
s = ZmqSubConnection(zf, e)
s.subscribe("")

def doPrint(message, tag=None):
    zlib.decompress(message)
s.gotMessage = doPrint
```

I'm getting this:

```
        Traceback (most recent call last):
          File "/home/gtaylor/.virtualenvs/thundercuddles/lib/python2.7/site-packages/Twisted-12.0.0-py2.7-linux-x86_64.egg/twisted/python/context.py", line 118, in callWithContext
            return self.currentContext().callWithContext(ctx, func, *args, **kw)
          File "/home/gtaylor/.virtualenvs/thundercuddles/lib/python2.7/site-packages/Twisted-12.0.0-py2.7-linux-x86_64.egg/twisted/python/context.py", line 81, in callWithContext
            return func(*args,**kw)
          File "/home/gtaylor/.virtualenvs/thundercuddles/lib/python2.7/site-packages/Twisted-12.0.0-py2.7-linux-x86_64.egg/twisted/internet/posixbase.py", line 586, in _doReadOrWrite
            why = selectable.doRead()
          File "/home/gtaylor/.virtualenvs/thundercuddles/lib/python2.7/site-packages/txZMQ/connection.py", line 165, in doRead
            log.callWithLogger(self, self.messageReceived, message)
        --- <exception caught here> ---
          File "/home/gtaylor/.virtualenvs/thundercuddles/lib/python2.7/site-packages/Twisted-12.0.0-py2.7-linux-x86_64.egg/twisted/python/log.py", line 84, in callWithLogger
            return callWithContext({"system": lp}, func, *args, **kw)
          File "/home/gtaylor/.virtualenvs/thundercuddles/lib/python2.7/site-packages/Twisted-12.0.0-py2.7-linux-x86_64.egg/twisted/python/log.py", line 69, in callWithContext
            return context.call({ILogContext: newCtx}, func, *args, **kw)
          File "/home/gtaylor/.virtualenvs/thundercuddles/lib/python2.7/site-packages/Twisted-12.0.0-py2.7-linux-x86_64.egg/twisted/python/context.py", line 118, in callWithContext
            return self.currentContext().callWithContext(ctx, func, *args, **kw)
          File "/home/gtaylor/.virtualenvs/thundercuddles/lib/python2.7/site-packages/Twisted-12.0.0-py2.7-linux-x86_64.egg/twisted/python/context.py", line 81, in callWithContext

          File "/home/gtaylor/.virtualenvs/thundercuddles/lib/python2.7/site-packages/txZMQ/pubsub.py", line 63, in messageReceived
            self.gotMessage(*reversed(message[0].split('\0', 1)))
          File "server.tac", line 94, in doPrint
            zlib.decompress(buf, -1)
        zlib.error: Error -2 while preparing to decompress data: inconsistent stream state

I started comparing the size of the 'message' variables in both versions of the module, and it appeared that the txZMQ one is about half the size of the gevent_pyzmq one. Furthermore, it looks like a bunch of data is being stuffed in the 'tag' variable randomly. Oddly enough, len(message+tag) always ends up being one character short of what is in my gevent version.

Any ideas?

issue_title: Use pyenv for Python version management?
issue_body:
The Python version management features of pew currently depend on the maintainer's [fork of pythonz](https://github.com/berdario/pythonz/tree/bd), which is currently out of date. Furthermore, the upstream pythonz repo hasn't seen an update in some time.

pyenv is actively maintained and a more complete solution for Python version management. Perhaps pew should use pyenv for `pew install`, `pew list_pythons`, etc.

Another option is to deprecate Python version management from pew. It seems from #152 and #146 that users prefer to have fewer required dependencies, so perhaps the correct path is to eventually remove the feature altogether.

issue_title: Add python_requires
issue_body:
`python_requires` allows you to bundle actual machine-readable metadata about what Python versions are supported in your distributions. It is best to add it *before* you need it, since `pip` falls back to the most recent version of a library where your python version meets the requirements, so you don't want to add this *after* your support matrix changes.

I think this project needs to add this to its `setup()`:

```python
python_requires=">=2.7, !=3.0.*, !=3.1.*, !=3.2.*,!=3.3.*,!=3.4.*"
```

Note that you must also build with a recent version of setuptools and upload your packages with twine in order for PyPI  to respect it.

issue_title: Extension API
issue_body:

But internally there's an emerging signature of what plugins to glom's recursion could look like. I think after #7 adds validation we could look at turning that API into a `GlomContext` object and exposing this.

issue_title: [improvement] ACL Design Pattern - ability to define ACLs in a flexible way
issue_body:
Hey,

I open this issue, to have a little brainstorm about what could be done related to facelift the ACL levels in will. I think I'm not the only one who would need this. I'd like to discuss a few ACL designs. I'm pretty open to implement it as well.

I personally love this approach. Instead using the following existing ACL:

```python
@respond_to("something", admin_only=True)
```

I'd rather go with a parameter called `acl` which would accept a [set](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#set) as a parameter. like this:

```python
@respond_to("something", acl={"infra_admin", "infra_ops", "infra_developer"})
```

To keep it compatible, we can keep the `admin_only=True` as well and add the `acl` as an optional parameter.

The definition of the ACLs for my example would look like this:

```python
ACL_INFRA_OPS = []
ACL_INFRA_ADMIN = []
ACL_INFRA_DEVELOPER = []
```
If an ACL definition does not exists, simply "skip" that ACL check and notify the requester, that the given ACL group does not exists.

What do you think?

issue_title: Are you interested in contributions?
issue_body:
I finally picked up `greenery` this afternoon, and have had a lot of fun with it.  Before I spend much more time on it though I thought I'd check if you're interested in contributions:

- I wrote an alternative string-to-lego parser which leans heavily on the [CPython `sre_parse` module](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/sre_parse.py) - https://github.com/qntm/greenery/compare/master...Zac-HD:pyregex.  It supports every construct that can be `re.compile`d, but has somewhat worse errors at the moment (eg no context given when bailing on a groupref).  Also needs more testing for eg repeats :smile: 

- I also wrote some property-based tests with [Hypothesis](https://hypothesis.works/), https://github.com/qntm/greenery/compare/master...Zac-HD:property-tests.  This has already turned up a few bugs of the form `x != parse(str(x))` for some lego object x, but there's little point looking for more if you don't consider this a bug worth fixing.  (I originally started this on the parser branch, but similar problems seem to exist on master too)

Either way, thanks for a great little library and a fun evening poking at it!

Once the NEW process is completed, it takes about 10 days for the package to trickle down into "testing", which will become the next Debian release. This means your software will eventually be shipped in a "long term support" release, which last from three to five years, according to the [current statistics](https://wiki.debian.org/DebianReleases#Production_Releases).

If you are interested in maintaining the package yourself upstream, that is definitely possible. You can either upload the package yourself if you are a Debian member or I can act as a sponsor when a new release is published.

This is just a notification, but can also act as a coordination point for upstream packaging if you wish to followup on this. otherwise, feel free to close this issue whenever you like.

issue_title: give logging backend more structure
issue_body:
I think there are two use cases for the logging backend:

1. A ops person or developer person is watching the logs go sailing by and is keeping an eye on certain metrics. This is good for local development.
2. Someone writes a log parser and it's pulling out and keeping track of metrics to report on.

The latter would be a lot easier if the logged lines were better structured and easier to tokenize.

This issue covers fixing that. Maybe the best idea is to mimic the format we use in the cloudwatch backend?

issue_title: formatters.format_ad_timestamp(): Local variable 'timestamp' referenced before assignment
issue_body:
As you can see [here](https://github.com/cannatag/ldap3/blob/master/ldap3/protocol/formatters/formatters.py#L107) the local variable `timestamp` is used in the exception handler, where it is not defined.

Running in the exception handler raises an `UnboundLocalError`. This happens for example if you want to pass a `datetime` object to the Active Directory.

issue_title: Confirm integer marhsalling/unmarshalling
issue_body:
Creating this ticket as a follow-up of a not 100% clear matter identified in https://github.com/cocagne/txdbus/pull/65#issuecomment-306827838.

Facts:
* DBus has several int types, singed/unsigned, from 8- to 64- bit wide.
* Python 3 unified Python int and longs into a common int. In either case, these are arbitrariliy long and may not fit in a 64-bit wide representation.
* With #11 and associated tickets some changes have been made which may affect this.

What I'm trying to account for:
* This code appropriately marshals/unmarshals whichever ints (or longs, in Python 2) into the appropriate DBus types or "fails" in a documented way (i.e.: when marshalling a value that does not fit the destination DBus type).

Maybe this is all correct, maybe not. Just leaving this here for future reference and to be checked by someone (me, who knows?) in the future.

Plan:
* Ensure tests exist for all combinations of DBus integer types and valid/invalid Python integers for each of those.

I am trying to implement the front and back car detector for traffic signs. The problem is that sometimes signs are facing the camera (with a square aspect ratio) and sometimes are seen from the side (as a rectangle).

Since I saw the detector is having a hard time training (and gave a recall of 0), and since it was hard to tell the limit of when a sign from the side is different enough to constitute a different class, I decided to just train a separate "side view" detector by just going through the xml file and adding ignore = '1' to each label that is closely a square.

I keep getting this message (with varying values for height and width, that as a side note do not actually exist in my xml label file):

"Warning, ignoring object.  We encountered a truth rectangle with a width and height of 171 and 166.  The image pyramid and sliding windows can't output a rectangle of this shape.  This is either because (1) the final layer's features have too large of a stride across the image, limiting the possible locations the sliding window can search or (2) because the rectangle's aspect ratio is too different from the best matching detection window, which has a width and height of 30 and 65."

Does this refer to the ignored boxes or is it another mistake I missed?

Thanks!

issue_title: EC Signature allows to use ssh pub key format but HMAC doesn't check invalid secret key.
issue_body: The ECDSA algorithm allows to use ssh public key format `ecdsa-sha2` but HMAC doesn't check invalid secret key. If users use ECDSA algorithm and they don't identify algorithm on decode, the attacker can tamper algorithm to HS. Then the server will use ECDSA public key with HMAC secret key.

issue_title: Ramp up the number of links per session
issue_body:
The AMQP 1.0 standard limits the number or sessions per connection to 64K.   However the number of links per session has a very high limit (2^32-1).

Pyngus should aggressively use pre existing sessions when opening new links.

issue_title: Error in Metropolis acceptance/rejection in MCMC?
issue_body:
Noticed some strange behaviour in an MCMC run, so took a look at the code in the master branch and to me it appears as if this is not following the standard Metropolis algorithm. Specifically, in lines 135-140:

```
like = self.postprocessing(self.iter, randompar, simulations, chains=cChain)
logMetropHastRatio = np.abs(self.bestlike[cChain])/np.abs(like)
u = np.random.uniform(low=0.3, high=1)
if logMetropHastRatio>1.0 or logMetropHastRatio>u:
    self.update_mcmc_status(randompar,like,simulations,cChain)   
    self.accepted[cChain] += 1  # monitor acceptance
```

If I understand the rest of the code right, `self.bestlike[cChain]` is the likelihood of the current point (which self.update_mcmc_status(writes to) and `like` is the likelihood of the proposal point.

- Would the acceptance probability not normally be calculated as `(likelihood of proposal) / (likelihood of current)`, i.e. the other way around  compared to here?
- For likelihood functions that return a loglikelihood would the acceptance ratio not be calculated as `(loglikelihood of proposal) - (loglikelihood of current)` ?
- Should the value of `u` be ~U(0, 1) rather than ~U(0.3, 1) ?

Is this a mistake in the code, or am I simply misunderstanding the way it works?

issue_title: Definition string contains cross-references
issue_body:
Hi again,

I'm importing the OBO file from NCIT, and when I extract the definition, I get the whole string unprocessed (i.e. entry.desc contains everything after the quotes). Here's an example entry:

```
[Term]
id: NCIT:C72909
name: Pentopril
def: "A non-sulfhydryl angiotensin converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor with antihypertensive activity. As a prodrug, pentopril is hydrolyzed into its active form pentoprilat, which competitively inhibits ACE, thereby blocking the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II. This prevents the potent vasoconstrictive actions of angiotensin II and results in vasodilation. Pentoprilat also decreases angiotensin II-induced aldosterone secretion by the adrenal cortex, which leads to an increase in sodium excretion and subsequently increases water outflow." [] {http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/NCIT_P378="NCI"}
```

So according to the spec (https://owlcollab.github.io/oboformat/doc/GO.format.obo-1_4.html#S.2.2), a "def" entry can consist of a string and a dbxref term. This is done correctly for the synonyms, could you do it for the definition as well?

issue_title: Column slicing issues
issue_body:
When attempting the following code
```
import fitsio
f = fitsio.FITS('spec-3874-55280-0067.fits')
clolumn = f[2]['SUBCLASS']
```
column is returned as
```
file: ~/spec-3874-55280-0067.fits
  extension: 2
  type: BINARY_TBL
  rows: 1
  column subset:
    CLASS               S6  array[6]
    SUBCLASS           S21  array[21]
    U                   f4  array[5]
    L                   f8  
    B                   f8  
```
and attempting to take column[:] crashes iPython.

If I use
```
columns = f[2]['CLASS','SUBCLASS']
```
returns
```
file: ~/spec-3874-55280-0067.fits
  extension: 2
  type: BINARY_TBL
  rows: 1
  column subset:
    CLASS               S6  array[6]
    SUBCLASS           S21  array[21]
```
and columns[:] works fine in this case.

It looks like when specifying a single column, fitsio searches for any subset of the given column name.

issue_title: use `pandas.read_csv` rather than `DataFrame.from_csv`
issue_body: `from_csv` is deprecated, so creates a lot of deprecation warnings in the code. Additionally, `read_csv` is 46x to 490x faster than `from_csv`. There are small changes to the interface, described [here](https://stackoverflow.com/a/26495839).

issue_title: Wrongly reporting remote name within Service.add
issue_body:
```
% git repo hub add fubar/test origin
Successfully added `fubar/test` as remote named `github`
% git remote -v
all	git@github.com:fubar/test (fetch)
all	git@github.com:fubar/test (push)
origin	git@github.com:fubar/test (fetch)
origin	git@github.com:fubar/test (push)
```

should be:

```
% git repo hub add fubar/test origin
Successfully added `fubar/test` as remote named `origin`
```

as reported in #67.

issue_title: We should relax the max-line-length pep8 check for python files

Although I think the line limit is necessary to make codes readable, it shouldn't be strictly required since there are instances (like this) that longer codes brings benefits. issue_title: sonify.chords, unique times strikes again

There appears to be a bug in sonify.chords stemming from running unique on time events.  It's difficult to reproduce, since it boils down to numerical precision issues, but the resulting artifact is that some chords do not get sonified.

I've gisted a [notebook](https://gist.github.com/829701891958b7f9ae41) to illustrate the problem, but it depends on having the jams-sonify branch and beatles isophonics data, so it may take some effort to reproduce outside of my environment.  Still, I'm pretty sure this is caused by our old buddy [unique](https://github.com/craffel/mir_eval/blob/master/mir_eval/sonify.py#L213).

issue_title: a.intersect(b, wao=True) returns invalid BedTool when b has more than 3 columns
issue_body:
If you intersect a BedTool with a 4 column BedTool using wao, the result has different field counts depending on if there were hits:
```

chr1 1074355 1074747 b'''
b = '''chr1 1084455 1084647 a                     
chr1 1094355 1094747 b'''
a_bed = pybedtools.BedTool(a, from_string=True)

test_bed = a_bed.intersect(b_bed, wao=True)
test_bed.field_count()
```
Gives:
`AssertionError: {9, 10}`
With these fields:
```
['chr1', '1084355', '1084747', 'a', 'chr1', '1084455', '1084647', 'a', '192']
['chr1', '1074355', '1074747', 'b', '.', '-1', '-1', '', '.', '0']
```
So entry 7 in the last row is empty and extraneous. This is on pybedtools v0.7.10.

issue_title: usages() finds wrong uses in assignments
issue_body:
Take the following source:

``` Python
def newval(bla):
    return bla + 1


def foo(bar):
    bar = bar + 1
    return bar


def baz(bar):
    bar = newval(bar)
    return bar
```

In `foo`, calling `usages` on the left-hand side `bar` of the assignment correctly returns the locations for the left-hand side `bar` and the `bar` being returned, and not the `bar` argument and `bar` on the right-hand side.

In `baz`, calling `usages` on the left-hand side `bar` of the assignment incorrectly returns the argument to `newval` on the right-hand side in addition to the left-hand side and return arguments.

In `baz`, calling `usages` on the argument to `newval` actually returns _only_ the function parameter, not even the argument to `newval`.

Reproduction:

``` Python
SOURCE = """\
def newval(bla):
    return bla + 1


def foo(bar):
    bar = bar + 1
    return bar


def baz(bar):
    bar = newval(bar)
    return bar
"""

import jedi

print("bar in foo is correct")
s = jedi.Script(source=SOURCE, line=6, column=4)
print("Wanted: {}".format([(6, 4), (7, 11)]))
print("Got   : {}".format([(u.line, u.column) for u in s.usages()]))
print("")

print("bar in baz is incorrect")
s = jedi.Script(source=SOURCE, line=11, column=4)
print("Wanted: {}".format([(11, 4), (12, 11)]))
print("Got   : {}".format([(u.line, u.column) for u in s.usages()]))
print("")

print("bar in baz is incorrect")
s = jedi.Script(source=SOURCE, line=11, column=17)
print("Wanted: {}".format([(10, 8), (11, 17)]))
print("Got   : {}".format([(u.line, u.column) for u in s.usages()]))
print("")
```

issue_title: Freezing of custom datetime classes
issue_body:
Custom date times are not frozen when using freeze_time.

Example:
```python
from datetime import datetime
from freezegun import freeze_time

class CustomDatetime(datetime):
  pass

with freeze_time('2018-05-30'):
  print(datetime.now())   # correctly outputs 2018-05-30 00:00:00
  print(CustomDatetime.now())   # invalid output of current timestamp
```

I have encountered this issue when using faker library which uses a [custom datetime](https://github.com/joke2k/faker/blob/master/faker/utils/datetime_safe.py).

A similar use case would be aliases for release groups.
Certain subtitles of 1 release group will always be in sync with another release group (example: LOL and DIMENSION)

This is certainly useful where subliminal is used in library mode and certain criteria must match before a subtitle is downloaded. If for example a subtitle is available under 'LOL' but the not under 'DIMENSION', it would be nice that 'DIMENSION' is also matched for 'LOL'. 
So making it possible to provide 'aliases' for certain video fields would be a good idea.

issue_title: Dump intermediate certificate
issue_body:
Let's Encrypt doesn't guarantee the use of a specific intermediate certificate for signing. Therefore it's currently not possible for a user of `acme-tiny` to create a `chain.pem` and `fullchain.pem` without the risk of breaking his setup when Let's Encrypt (suddenly) switches to `Let’s Encrypt Authority X2`. To solve this problem, [a ACME server must send a `Link: <https://example.com/acme/ca-cert>;rel="up";title="issuer"` header](https://letsencrypt.github.io/acme-spec/#rfc.section.6.6.p.9) when [retrieving a signed certificate](https://github.com/diafygi/acme-tiny/blob/fcb7cd6f66e951eeae76ff2f48d8ad3e40da37ef/acme_tiny.py#L156).

A possible solution I can think of is, that `acme-tiny` follows this link, appends all intermediate certificates to the signed certificate and therefore actually outputs/returns a `fullchain.pem`.

issue_title: sslyze fails to run plugins when only a few ciphers are accepted by the server
issue_body:
To my understanding, enumerating ciphers and running the plugins is handled differently regarding the ciphers provided by the client:

utils/SSLyzeSSLConnection.py:
```python
# https://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=665452
sslConn.set_cipher_list('HIGH:MEDIUM:-aNULL:-eNULL:-3DES:-SRP:-PSK:-CAMELLIA')
```

plugins/PluginOpenSSLCipherSuites.py:
```python
sslClient.set_cipher_list('ALL:COMPLEMENTOFALL')
```

The comment clearly states why this is done (problem with long requests). However, if a SSL service is configured to only allow a few ciphers, i.e. 3DES, enumerating the ciphers works, but all plugins will fail with a handshake exception.

My suggestion would be to catch the handshake failure and to retry the connection with all ciphers ('ALL:COMPLEMENTOFALL') or all ciphers which have not been tried in the previous request (low, null, 3des, ...).

Thanks!

issue_title: Improve documentation and add advisory_xact_lock
issue_body:
You can not protect database operations inside the advisory_lock context if you are running under Django transaction Middleware. The advisory_lock context is still good if you want to protect concurrent access to other resources (we use it to avoid stampede on some external resources)

For example, this code is as good as not locked:

``` python
with advisory_lock(42):
    obj = MyObject.get(id=42)
    obj.accessed +=1
    obj.save()
```

If you do not commit _inside_ the context, then with two workers you can get the following behavior:
- A takes lock
- B wait on lock
- A read obj, accessed = 0
- A write accessed = 1 (change not committed)
- A release lock
- B takes lock (this goes very fast since B was waiting for it)
- B read obj, accessed = 0 (A change still not committed : it takes time to build the response)
- A commits
- B writes accessed = 1
- B commits

We had a very ugly bug in production because we were on the wrong impression our databases transactions were protected inside the advisory_lock context.

We had to write own py2/3 compatible version of this, that I share with you here under MIT license https://gist.github.com/lionel-panhaleux/bd62ade43afe57e6f365 .

issue_title: nlp does not include "now" in the phrase "1 year from now"
issue_body:
I use `nlp` to extract date phrases from user-provided text, leaving just the remaining text. In this use case, it is fairly apparent when `nlp` does not extend the phrase to include all terms. When processed through `nlp`, the phrase *one year from now* does not include the word *now* while *one year from today* works as expected.
```
> python -c "import parsedatetime;print parsedatetime.Calendar().nlp('1 year from now')"
((datetime.datetime(2016, 5, 29, 22, 22, 39), 1, 0, 11, '1 year from'),)
> python -c "import parsedatetime;print parsedatetime.Calendar().nlp('1 year from today')"
((datetime.datetime(2016, 5, 29, 9, 0), 1, 0, 17, '1 year from today'),)
```

Very minor detail, just wanted to make note so that I can fix it when I get a chance. The dates seem to be correct, differentiating in an exact by-the-second time from "now" versus a date with a morning time from "today"

issue_title: Invalid dumps of non-finite complex Nums
issue_body:
Related to #82 and #85, `ast.Num`s containing non-finite complex numbers will be dumped as their reprs instead of legal Python expressions that evaluate to the right floating-point constructs.

    import ast, astor

    x = (1e1000 - 1e1000) - 1e1000j

    print(x)
    print(astor.code_gen.to_source(ast.Module([ast.Expr(ast.Num(x))])))

Both lines print as `(nan-infj)`.

issue_title: DefaultConfigFileParser's serialize does not work with lists of ints/floats
issue_body:
The newer version uses [str.join(_iterable_)](https://docs.python.org/2/library/stdtypes.html#str.join) where the elements in _iterable_ must be strings.
```
def serialize(self, items):
        """Does the inverse of config parsing by taking parsed values and
        converting them back to a string representing config file contents.
        """
        r = StringIO()
        for key, value in items.items():
            if type(value) == list:
                # handle special case of lists
                value = "["+", ".join(value)+"]"
            r.write("%s = %s\n" % (key, value))
        return r.getvalue()
```
Compare with older version from https://pypi.python.org/pypi/ConfigArgParse:
```
    def serialize(self, items):
        """Does the inverse of config parsing by taking parsed values and
        converting them back to a string representing config file contents.

        Args:
            items: an OrderedDict with items to be written to the config file
        Returns:
            contents of config file as a string
        """
        r = StringIO()
        for key, value in items.items():
            r.write("%s = %s\n" % (key, value))
        return r.getvalue()
```
Why the change?  It was actually working fine for me before.

issue_title: deployment to github releases from appveyor fails
issue_body:
The upload of the generated wheels to Github Releases requires that the admin of this repo, @greginvm, updates the `auth_token` field in the .appveyor.yml file with his Github API key, as explained in the inline comments in that file.

I am only a collaborator so I can push tags, but can't generate a new API token, nor I can encrypt it using the Appveyor account that is linked to this repo (that also needs @greginvm privileges).

I am thinking that maybe it's not worth keeping this "upload to github" feature. We simply need the wheels to be uploaded to PyPI, where pip can download them from. Having them on Github is a bonus but not really required.

So if we agree, I can set up Appveyor and Travis to upload the wheels directly to PyPI using my own PyPI credentials (i have access to the pyclipper PyPI account so it should work).

issue_title: IntInterval and FloatInterval crash when input is not of the same type.
issue_body:
Verified both with Python 2 and Python 3.

If I want to check if a specific number is in an interval and the number type does not match that of the interval an exception is raised (check examples below).

I can except the case when `float` is not in `IntInterval` because the floating point numbers does not belong to the set of integers, but then the result should be `False` not an exception.
As for 'int' in 'FloatInterval' as the real numbers set include the integer numbers set then it should return true no matter if we pass integer or float.

P.S. I would be happy to work on a fix )

issue_title: Reduce polling for XF (SpaceBattles/SufficientVelocity/etc) sites
issue_body:
I've had a case of someone using FFF to hit the SpaceBattles some ~30000 times a day for updates.

It looks like you aren't caching HTTP redirects from the thread id to the 'canonical' url, and it looks possible that your app can send multiple simultaneous requests for the same content.

It appears to be the same content being requested in a tight loop, over an over again. SpaceBattles's loadbalancer health checks hit a vastly cheaper monitoring point less times per day.

I'm aware you can't enforce this on the user's of your software, or even force people to update, but some sane defaults or warnings about being overly aggressive would be appreciated.

issue_title: Please switch the order of the removal of stopwords and the removal of 's in WordCloud.process_text()
issue_body:
Right now, when the word cloud is generated, the order in which the word list is filtered is as such (see wordcloud.py, lines 554-560):
```
# remove stopwords
words = [word for word in words if word.lower() not in stopwords]
# remove 's
words = [word[:-2] if word.lower().endswith("'s") else word
         for word in words]
# remove numbers
words = [word for word in words if not word.isdigit()]
```
The problem with this order is that if one adds a word in the stopwords list, say _pipe_ for instance, and that word also appears as _pipe's_ in the text, the instances of _pipe_ will be found and discarded, but the instances of _pipe's_ will remain, will be stripped of their trailing 's, and will end up appearing in the word cloud as _pipe_ anyway! This is very surprising, and most probably not the behaviour one would want. In fact, it took me quite a while to discover why the words I'd explicitly put in the stopwords list were still appearing in the word clouds I was creating!

While it is possible to work around this issue by adding _pipe's_ to the stopwords list, I can't think of a use case where one would explicitly add a word in the stopwords list but would still want it to appear in the word cloud if it had appeared in the text with a trailing 's, and this could easily be solved by a quick reorder of the lines in wordcloud.py as such:
```
# remove 's
words = [word[:-2] if word.lower().endswith("'s") else word
         for word in words]
# remove stopwords
words = [word for word in words if word.lower() not in stopwords]
# remove numbers
words = [word for word in words if not word.isdigit()]
```
What do you think?

issue_title: GTK 3 example: issues with mixed GTK 2 and GTK 3 symbols in libcef.so (v54)
issue_body:
The current gtk.py example uses GTK 3, as this is what PyGObject supports (Issue #253). For GTK 2 pygtk library must be used.

Noting this, as there might be an issue with cefpython and GTK 3. In setup.py the cefpython module links to such libraries:
```
'gobject-2.0',
'glib-2.0',
'gtk-x11-2.0',
```

Looks like 'gtk-x11-2.0' is GTK 2.0 and you cannot use GTK 2 and GTK 3 in the same process. I've realized this during update to Chrome v54 (#260), as libcef.so linked to GTK 2.0 and it caused this error (reported on the [CEF Forum #14596](https://magpcss.org/ceforum/viewtopic.php?f=6&t=14596)):
```
Gtk-ERROR **: GTK+ 2.x symbols detected. Using GTK+ 2.x and GTK+ 3 in the same process is not supported
```

Maybe rename pygobject example to `gtk-3.0.py` and refactor the old pygtk_.py example and name it `gtk-2.0.py`?

issue_title: set_wwr() for individual surfaces
issue_body:
It is often necessary to apply different window to wall ratios for different blocks (e.g. ground floor with fully glazed retail, whereas first and above are punched windows for resi), zones (especially in multi-level mixed-use), or even individual surfaces.

To my understanding, the current code only supports applying set_wwr() to the entire building, with or without orientational mapping.

Ideally it would be possible to set the window to wall ratio(s) for individual surfaces (or lists of surfaces). This would allow a surfaces (or list thereof) to be selected for whatever criteria may be applicable to the individual project (or workflow).

_Background information; I tend to take highly detailed models (created with other tools) and modify these to obtain reference models for regulatory compliance modelling. As part of this process I typically remove all actuals windows and shading components, and apply % based glazing per block._

Kind regards,
Franz

issue_title: Methods `fileno()` for compatiability with `selectors`
issue_body:
The [`selectors` module](https://docs.python.org/3/library/selectors.html#module-selectors) can register objects which export a `fileno()` method which returns the file descriptor integer.

This is already exported by, for example, `mqd` and `fd`, but maybe a method for `fileno()` could also be added?

A current workaround is to extend the classes from `posix_ipc` and add the method.

issue_title: Colors: Default style suggestions
issue_body:
Added `default_styles` in `colorlib/names.py`, and user stylesheets can be loaded too. Any suggestions (from anyone) for the default styles? Either new styles or different colors/attributes for current styles. Here's the current one that I quickly threw together:

```python
default_styles = dict(
    warn="fg red",
    title="fg cyan underline bold",
    fatal="fg red bold",
    highlight="bg yellow",
    info="fg blue",
    success="fg green",
    )
```

Note: to quickly test these, for example `warn`, simply use the following command on the command line:
```bash
$ python -m plumbum.colors warn
```

You can also test the style before submitting it:
```bash
$ python -m plumbum.colors fg red bg 123 underline bold
```

And reset:
```bash
$ python -m plumbum.colors
```

Also, anything in the library, including 24 bit color, is available, though sticking to a smaller subset of colors (8, 16, or 256) is more universally usable. For example, colorama only supports the first 16.

issue_title: Allow override Settings.database property via setattr
issue_body:
I would be nice to have a legal way to globally change default example database and only. The most intuitive way seems to be:

```
import hypothesis
import hypothesis.database

hypothesis.Settings.default.database = hypothesis.database.SQLiteBackend()
```

Gives:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 4, in <module>
    hypothesis.Settings.default.database = hypothesis.database.SQLiteBackend()
  File "/usr/local/opt/virtualenv/aiocouchdb/lib/python3.3/site-packages/hypothesis/settings.py", line 210, in __setattr__
    raise AttributeError(u'No such setting %s' % (name,))
AttributeError: No such setting database
```

Currently, it's possible only through private property:

```
hypothesis.Settings.default._database = hypothesis.database.SQLiteBackend()
```

Or by overriding all default settings with new instance:
```
hypothesis.Settings.default = Settings(database=hypothesis.database.SQLiteBackend())
```

What may accidentally erase some other changes to default settings made somewhere else.

issue_title: Failure in extract_lambda_source() with tuple unpacking in lambdas
issue_body:
The following code:

```python
from hypothesis import strategies as st
st.tuples(st.just('a'), st.just('b')).map(lambda (a, b): a + b)
```

fails with this error:

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/hypothesis/searchstrategy/strategies.py", line 530, in __repr__
    self.pack)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/hypothesis/internal/reflection.py", line 265, in get_pretty_function_description
    result = extract_lambda_source(f)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/hypothesis/internal/reflection.py", line 208, in extract_lambda_source
    if_confused = 'lambda %s: <unknown>' % (', '.join(args),)
TypeError: sequence item 0: expected string or Unicode, list found
```

As far as I can tell, the problem is that the 'args' field of the argspec is not always a list of strings; in particular, when tuple unpacking is used like this, it can be a nested list of lists. Calling join with such an object then gives the error above.

issue_title: blob constructor is not consistent across browsers
issue_body:
The good news very welcome indeed is that it works on all browsers.
Internet Explorer has a SaveAs option which allows the user to save the file where it should be in the first place.
All others dump the file into the downloads folder (beats me why the new file API designers think placing the file in downloads is more secure than putting into the place where the file actually belongs) 
Currently IE (going back several versions) also supports document.execcommand.saveAS which is very easy to use. The bad news is that IE is going away sometime in the future.
It sure would be nice if all browsers reacted consistently to the blob parameters and would let us store our data where we need it.
I am  not sure if all the great work done so far with FileSaver.js can overcome the above obstacles which belong in the WWW consortium. 
Thank for the good work

issue_title: TypeError: __new__() takes 5 positional arguments but 6 were given
issue_body:
I was trying to configure electrumx for florincoin and edited following code in tx.py to handle comments
```
def read_tx(self):
        '''Return a (Deserialized TX, TX_HASH) pair.

        The hash needs to be reversed for human display; for efficiency
        we process it in the natural serialized order.
        '''
        start = self.cursor
        return Tx(
            self._read_le_int32(),  # version
            self._read_inputs(),    # inputs
            self._read_outputs(),   # outputs
            self._read_le_uint32(),  # locktime
            **self._read_scripts()    # comment**
        ), double_sha256(self.binary[start:self.cursor])
```
and defined _read_scripts() as
```
def _read_scripts(self):
        read_script = self._read_script
        return [read_output() for i in range(self._read_varint())]

    def _read_script(self):
        return TxOutput(
            self._read_le_int64(),
            self._read_varbytes(),
        )
```
But I am getting TypeError: __new __() takes 5 positional arguments but 6 were given

issue_title: Enhancement: Report daemon version in peers RPC
issue_body:
On testnet if you have a segwit wallet and are connected to a non-segwit server, transactions are rejected with a cryptic error message:
```
---------------------------
Error
---------------------------
error: The transaction was rejected by network rules.  (16: bad-txns-vin-empty)
[]
---------------------------
OK   
---------------------------
```
The same transaction goes through when connected to a server using bitcoin 0.13.2.
This is really a client issue, but servers do not currently give clients enough information to handle this in a better way.

* DRF OpenAPI version: 1.3

issue_title: Feature Request: Setting Castling Rights
Saving tuples as keys using sqlitedict
issue_body:
This issue is more a new feature idea.

It would be nice to be able to use Python tuples as keys as you can do it in standard Python dicts. My idea is to do something like serialisation from tuple to string (for example via `user_defined_character.join(tuple)` or via some user defined conversion function). 

For querying there would be used just the same transformation function and for keys reconstruction there might be something like, either `key.split(user_defined_character)` or some other user defined conversion function.

issue_title: sometimes can't find uiobject but object do exists
issue_body:
By testing some app, I notice that sometimes I can see ui on screen, but uiautomator can't find the uiobject. When I dump window hierarchy to xml using code, seeing thing are difference from what I dump from adb uiautomatorviewer tool. The xml dumped from code, lesser element than dumped from uiautomatorviewer.
I find out that the dump difference is cause by the compressed argument. And, if I set compressed to false, the uiobject can be found by uiautomator again. I guess what I need is setCompressedLayoutHeirarchy method, but it is not expose by uiautomator. After review the server code I think I can use dump(compressed=False) to do this.
So, can it be a good idea to explicitly set compressed layout hierarchy on or off?
Thank you very much.

issue_title: Ability to have recursive schema
issue_body:
Today there's no easy syntax to have a recursive schema. The best way to do it is to have a wrapper like that:


```python
>>> import voluptuous
>>> def s2(v):
...     return s1(v)
...
>>> s1 = voluptuous.Schema({"abc": voluptuous.Any(s2, "def")})
>>> s1({"abc": {"abc": "def"}})
{'abc': {'abc': 'def'}}
```

issue_title: Python 2 Unicode escapes should be parsed in search pattern
issue_body:
In Python2, ur'\u0070', would be created as ur'p' when the string was instantiated.  In Python3, r'\u0070' is still `\\u0070`.  Because of this, Python3's Re library handles escaped Unicode notation, while Python2 does not.

The reason why this is a problem is that it leads to some inconsistencies. In Python3, you can construct a non-raw string like this: `'\\u0070'` and it will be treated just like `r'\u0070'`.  These are not equivalent in Python2. In backrefs, we parse case both literal unicode and `r'\u0070'` to apply casing.  This partly because this is how regex does things, but also because it makes sense as a user usually is using raw strings for replace patterns to avoid specifiying groups as `\\2` instead of the nicer `\2`, and may find it easier to use Unicode notation as well then trying to input literal Unicode.  Now on the surface this all behaves the same across all Python, but not when you use non-raw strings.

This mainly becomes a problem when you are manually constructing a string, you may use non raw string to create a string `'\\u0070'`, but while Python3 Re will parse this as Unicode, Python2 will not unless you are manually converting these.

In backrefs, we specifically handle `'\\u0070'` to apply casing and create consistency between Regex and Re, but since we don't case in search strings, we don't actually scan for non raw Unicode representations as we expected the regular expression engine to handle this, which it does, but only on Python3+.

So basically, we just want to handle scan for the `'\\u0070'` in non-raw search patterns as well.  This will keep behavior of `'\\Q\\u0070\\E'` consistent across Python versions.  Though `ur'\Q\u0070\E'` behavior will still be different for Python2 and will yield `'p'` instead of of `'\\\\u0070'`, but there is really nothing we can do about this. We can at least get behavior as close to the same as possible.

This is mainly so we can ensure that the Rummage project can send in a string from the GUI which will essentially be a non-raw string without having to reprocess the pattern looking for Unicode notation. It will still need to preprocess patterns intended for Re directly to ensure general Unicode is treated as Unicode.

issue_title: There is no tag for release 2.4
HidReport() class : bad self.__raw_report_size
issue_body:
HidReport() class initialisation starts with the following :
```c
    def __init__(self, hid_object, report_type, report_id):
        hid_caps = hid_object.hid_caps
        if report_type == HidP_Input:
            self.__raw_report_size = hid_caps.input_report_byte_length
        elif report_type == HidP_Output:
            self.__raw_report_size = hid_caps.output_report_byte_length
        elif report_type == HidP_Feature:
            self.__raw_report_size = hid_caps.feature_report_byte_length
        else:
            raise HIDError("Unsupported report type")
```
```self.__raw_report_size``` is badly set to one of :
- hid_caps.input_report_byte_length 
- hid_caps.output_report_byte_length 
- hid_caps.feature_report_byte_length.

These values are the max of each report type.

For example, if there are 2 Report IN, one of size 4 and one of size 8, all HidReport() of type HidP_Input will have ```self.__raw_report_size``` set to 8. This is not correct.

issue_title: --tor-control-port= is broken
issue_body:
rxcomm noticed that using `--tor-control-port=` is broken: using it always causes `wormhole` to fall back to using the default SOCKS port.

It appears that `txtorcon.connect(tor_control_port=)` doesn't accept a string, only one of:

* an endpoint instance
* a sequence of endpoint instances
* None, meaning try the usual suspects (`unix:/var/run/tor/control`, `tcp:127.0.0.1:9051`, `tcp:127.0.0.1:9151`)

So the fix is probably just having `wormhole.tor_manager.get_tor` render the argv value into an endpoint with `endpoints.clientFromString()` before passing it into `txtorcon.connect()`.

issue_title: Marker file prevents symbolic link from being added back
issue_body:
When we have a directory structure and remove part of it in one layer and add a symlink to removed directory in other layer -- this symlink is skipped. For example:

```
FROM busybox:1.24
RUN mkdir /dir
RUN touch /dir/a
RUN touch /dir/b
RUN mkdir /dir/dir
RUN touch /dir/dir/file
RUN mv /dir/dir /newdir
RUN ln -s /newdir /dir/dir
```

When we squash two latest layers then the `dir/.wh.dir` marker file is found which prevents the symlinks from being added to the squashed image because the target of this symlink is the same as the marker files which suggests that it should be skipped.

issue_title: Implement a GeneralOptimizer class for new topologies
issue_body:
As proposed in #147 we could implement a new GeneralOptimizer class in addition to the two existing GlobalBest and LocalBest classes. In this class, it will then be possible to select a custom topology for the optimization. We could also include a foundation for multilayer PSO (as proposed in #75).

The only issue I see is that a GeneralOptimizer might make the GlobalBest or LocalBest classes obsolete as it might optimize faster and/or run with less memory consumption especially if we implement higher-level topologies such as Von Neumann or Pyramid (#129).

issue_title: AttributeError: 'list' object has no attribute 'lstrip'
issue_body:
It works for the standard [cas](https://github.com/apereo/cas) server, But when I use this perfect library to integrate to my ids server, it shown the following error.

```python
127.0.0.1 - - [09/May/2018 10:26:27] "GET /login/?ticket=ST-43153-EpldxJPeJzBJQWqwx2ih1525832788741-9WDe-ids1 HTTP/1.1" 500 -
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\ProgramData\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\flask\app.py", line 1997, in __call__
    return self.wsgi_app(environ, start_response)
  File "C:\ProgramData\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\flask\app.py", line 1985, in wsgi_app
    response = self.handle_exception(e)
  File "C:\ProgramData\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\flask\app.py", line 1540, in handle_exception
    reraise(exc_type, exc_value, tb)
  File "C:\ProgramData\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\flask\_compat.py", line 33, in reraise
    raise value
  File "C:\ProgramData\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\flask\app.py", line 1982, in wsgi_app
    response = self.full_dispatch_request()
  File "C:\ProgramData\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\flask\app.py", line 1614, in full_dispatch_request
    rv = self.handle_user_exception(e)
  File "C:\ProgramData\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\flask\app.py", line 1517, in handle_user_exception
    reraise(exc_type, exc_value, tb)
  File "C:\ProgramData\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\flask\_compat.py", line 33, in reraise
    raise value
  File "C:\ProgramData\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\flask\app.py", line 1612, in full_dispatch_request
    rv = self.dispatch_request()
  File "C:\ProgramData\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\flask\app.py", line 1598, in dispatch_request
    return self.view_functions[rule.endpoint](**req.view_args)
  File "C:\ProgramData\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\flask_cas\routing.py", line 45, in login
    if validate(flask.session[cas_token_session_key]):
  File "C:\ProgramData\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\flask_cas\routing.py", line 128, in validate
    attributes["cas:memberOf"] = attributes["cas:memberOf"].lstrip('[').rstrip(']').split(',')
AttributeError: 'list' object has no attribute 'lstrip'
```

After going through the code, I find the error is this line `attributes["cas:memberOf"] = attributes["cas:memberOf"].lstrip('[').rstrip(']').split(',')`. The `attributes["cas:memberOf"]` is already a list object, no need to convert str to list.

So I change this line of code to
```python
if not isinstance(attributes["cas:memberOf"], list):
    attributes["cas:memberOf"] = attributes["cas:memberOf"].lstrip('[').rstrip(']').split(',')
```

Then it works.

issue_title: When code sent to do_execute is not complete, return invalid response
issue_body: The `do_is_complete` function prevents the Jupyter kernel from sending input until there's a valid, complete chunk of code. But when Atom sends code, there's no way to probe for more user input, so the selection goes directly to `do_execute`. Because of this, I can't assume that the code sent to `do_execute` is necessarily complete, and I need to check.

#### Description

An empty progressbar will be shown with N/A.

#### Code
```
from urllib.request import urlretrieve
from progressbar import ProgressBar, Percentage, Bar, Timer

pbar = ProgressBar(widgets=[Percentage(), Bar(), Timer()])

def dl_progress(count, block_size, total_size):
    pbar.update(int(count * block_size * 100 / total_size))

urlretrieve(uri, filepath, reporthook=dl_progress)

issue_title: getproctitle awkwardness
issue_body:
I've been working on a small class which is supposed to monitor processes and keep them up(restart when gone).
As an added bonus, that class allows one to pass in a process name which is then used by setproctitle to set that process name once the process is forked.

Here's the weird behavior I'm seeing.
The "process manager" is the parent process of all other started processes, I'm never calling setproctitle on it.
The code I'm using appends the name to the return of getproctitle.
If I start 3 processes and name them `p1`, `p2`, `p3`, the first final name is `<what's returned of getproctitle> p1`, the second, `<what's returned of getproctitle> p1 p2` and the third `<what's returned of getproctitle> p1 p2 p3`.

So even though the parent process never changes name, the child processes accumulate the appended names, why?

The processes are beeing forked, so they inherit the parents memory space, but this can't be it because the parent never changes names.

Oh, I'm using the multiprocessing module if that helps track down the behaviour.

Example python script:
```python
import time
import multiprocessing
import multiprocessing.util
import setproctitle

def after_fork(proc):
    setproctitle.setproctitle(setproctitle.getproctitle() + ' ' + proc.name)


def spin():
    while True:
        time.sleep(1)


processes = []
for n in range(3):
    proc = multiprocessing.Process(target=spin, name='foo {}'.format(n))
    multiprocessing.util.register_after_fork(proc, after_fork)
    processes.append(proc)
    proc.start()


while True:
    try:
        time.sleep(1)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        break


for proc in processes:
    proc.terminate()
```
PS Output:
```
vampas     819  1.6  0.0  33172  9720 pts/2    S+   10:11   0:00 python /tmp/test.py                 
vampas     958  0.0  0.0  33172  8232 pts/2    S+   10:11   0:00 python /tmp/test.py foo 0           
vampas     960  0.0  0.0  33172  8232 pts/2    S+   10:11   0:00 python /tmp/test.py foo 0 foo 1     
vampas     961  0.0  0.0  33172  8232 pts/2    S+   10:11   0:00 python /tmp/test.py foo 0 foo 1 foo 2
```

issue_title: Remove assert from base API client
issue_labels: [{'id': 676099992, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/hydrosquall/tiingo-python/labels/bug', 'name': 'bug', 'color': 'ee0701', 'default': True}, {'id': 713290911, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/hydrosquall/tiingo-python/labels/easy', 'name': 'easy', 'color': 'c2e0c6', 'default': False}, {'id': 727632614, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/hydrosquall/tiingo-python/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': 'd4c5f9', 'default': True}, {'id': 676099995, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/hydrosquall/tiingo-python/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': '128A0C', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
There is an assert statement in the API client code that presently tests for the existence of an API key. It's on line [63](https://github.com/hydrosquall/tiingo-python/blob/master/tiingo/api.py#L63)

However, assert statements are not good for production code because they can be overridden by runtime environment variables, i.e. `PYTHONOPTIMIZE`. To fix this:

- The assert statement should be removed, and replaced with a, i.e. `if variable`. If the test returns false, then a standard Python exception should be raised with a descriptive error message.
- A test should be added to the test suite that will complain if the API key is not present.

issue_title: review migrations in virtualenv
issue_body:
btw since I want to add fields on one of the model, I had a pb with a migration: when executing ./manage.py makemigrations it generated a migration for the schedule app, i.e. directly in my virtual env (that kind of things doesn't play well when rolling out to CI or production...). I was able to tweak it with the following, but it's not very optimal. Do you have plan to make things better? I can definitely write some code as well:

class CrossAppAddField(migrations.AddField):
    """
    Only way I found to migrate the model of another app.
    """
    def __init__(app_label, *args, **kwargs):
        self.app_label = app_label
        super(CrossAppAddField, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)

    def state_forwards(self, app_label, *args, **kwargs):
        """
        Override app_label.
        """
        return super(CrossAppAddField, self).state_forwards(self.app_label, *args, **kwargs)

    def database_forwards(self, app_label, *args, **kwargs):
        """
        Override app_label.
        """
        return super(CrossAppAddField, self).database_forwards(self.app_label, *args, **kwargs)

    def database_backwards(self, app_label, *args, **kwargs):  # pragma: no cover
        """
        Override app_label.
        """
        return super(CrossAppAddField, self).database_backwards(self.app_label, *args, **kwargs)
And then in one of my migrations, I would do: CrossAppAddField('schedule', model_name='event', name='event_type', field=models.CharField(...))

issue_body:

```
$ titlecase YAE NO
Yae No

$ titlecase ÝÆ ÑØ
ýæ ñø
```

These characters are taken from Latin1 range of Unicode, so characters most likely to have occurred in European languages.

The following diff shows this within the test framework.

```diff

issue_title: Runtime detection of optimized module
issue_body:
Hi all,

I was wondering if it will be possible to detect at runtime if the imported module is the native python version or instead the "compiled" one using pythran. 

This is a nice to have in production system as well as during development stages. The rationale is detecting at runtime if the current system is running or not with the support of the optimized version of your code. 

Currently this is possible using a snippet like:
```python
import mymodule

if not mymodule.__file__.endswith('.so'):
    print('loaded .py module')
```

I'm not sure this is the way to go but I was wondering if it may be possible to add a set of globals to the generated module. This will allows the users to quickly check if the optimized version is available or not (e.g. __PYTHRAN) and to inspect some metadata regarding the compilation process (e.g compiler version, flags, timestamp and hash of the original .py file).  Several examples can be found for these flags. The hash one can provide a quick way to check if a given compile module still refer to actual version of its .py counterpart.

For instance as pseudocode:
```python
import hashlib

def check_source(mymodule):
    import mymodule

    if not mymodule.__file__.endswith('.so'):
        return False

    h = hashlib.sha1()
    
    with open('mymodule.py') as fp:
         h.update(fp.read())
    
    return mymodule.__PYTHRAN_HASH == h.hexdigest()
```

Then on the command line:
```
$ pythran --check mymodule.py
>> Compiled module (mymodule.so) matches (mymodule.py) source version.
```

issue_title: Ignore entries from other `known_` sections?

For a Django project, I like to have all the `django` imports in its own section. I do this using isort's custom sections:

As it is, seed-isort-config ignores this setting, adding `django` to `known_third_party`, which takes precedence in isort.

issue_title: Wrong error: SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT must not be None when SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH set to "plaintext"
issue_body:
The auth token retrieval does not work in a testing environment with SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH being set to 'plaintext'. Using 'plaintext' makes sense for running unit tests (the crypt routines otherwise may significantly affect the duration it takes to run the tests).

Consider this small application:

``` python
from flask import Flask, render_template, g
from flask.ext.sqlalchemy import SQLAlchemy
import flask.ext.security
from flask.ext.security import UserMixin, RoleMixin
from flask.ext.security.registerable import register_user

auth_required = flask.ext.security.auth_token_required
app = Flask(__name__)
app.config['DEBUG'] = True
app.config['SECURITY_SEND_REGISTER_EMAIL'] = False
app.config['SECRET_KEY'] = 'a'
app.config['WTF_CSRF_ENABLED'] = False
app.config['SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI'] = "sqlite://"

db = SQLAlchemy(app)

roles_users = db.Table('roles_users',
    db.Column('user_id', db.Integer(), db.ForeignKey('user.id')),
    db.Column('role_id', db.Integer(), db.ForeignKey('role.id')))

class Role(db.Model, RoleMixin):
    id = db.Column(db.Integer(), primary_key=True)
    name = db.Column(db.String(80), unique=True)
    description = db.Column(db.String(255))

class User(db.Model, UserMixin):
    id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True)
    email = db.Column(db.String(255), unique=True)
    password = db.Column(db.String(255))
    active = db.Column(db.Boolean())
    confirmed_at = db.Column(db.DateTime())
    roles = db.relationship(
        'Role',
        secondary=roles_users,
        backref=db.backref('users', lazy='dynamic')
        )

user_datastore = flask.ext.security.SQLAlchemyUserDatastore(db, User, Role)
security = flask.ext.security.Security(app, user_datastore)

@app.route('/createtestuser')
def create_user():
    db.drop_all()
    db.create_all()
    register_user(email="mail1", password="testpassword1")
    return "OK"

app.run(debug=True, host="0.0.0.0")
```

Create a test user:

```
$ curl -i http://localhost:5000/createtestuser                                                                  HTTP/1.0 200 OK
Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8
Content-Length: 2
Set-Cookie: session=eyJfaWQiOnsiIGIiOiJNR0ZtTVRjd1lqVTRZMkppTjJFMk0yTm1NMll4TkRKbFltVXpNR0l5WW1FPSJ9fQ.CCu0Ww.dnadUzB93ML3oZt2EdrpbFS5bnA; HttpOnly; Path=/
Server: Werkzeug/0.10.4 Python/2.7.3
Date: Wed, 06 May 2015 14:19:07 GMT

OK
```

Attempt to retrieve auth token:

```
$ curl -i --data '{"password": "testpassword1", "email": "mail1"}' http://localhost:5000/login  -H "Content-Type: application/json"
```

Yields the following traceback:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1836, in __call__
    return self.wsgi_app(environ, start_response)
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1820, in wsgi_app
    response = self.make_response(self.handle_exception(e))
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1403, in handle_exception
    reraise(exc_type, exc_value, tb)
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1817, in wsgi_app
    response = self.full_dispatch_request()
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1477, in full_dispatch_request
    rv = self.handle_user_exception(e)
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1381, in handle_user_exception
    reraise(exc_type, exc_value, tb)
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1475, in full_dispatch_request
    rv = self.dispatch_request()
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1461, in dispatch_request
    return self.view_functions[rule.endpoint](**req.view_args)
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask_security/decorators.py", line 205, in wrapper
    return f(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask_security/views.py", line 79, in login
    if form.validate_on_submit():
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask_wtf/form.py", line 166, in validate_on_submit
    return self.is_submitted() and self.validate()
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask_security/forms.py", line 238, in validate
    if not verify_and_update_password(self.password.data, self.user):
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask_security/utils.py", line 137, in verify_and_update_password
    password = get_hmac(password)
  File "/xxx/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask_security/utils.py", line 110, in get_hmac
    'set to "%s"' % _security.password_hash)
RuntimeError: The configuration value `SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT` must not be None when the value of `SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH` is set to "plaintext"
```

This error message is contradictory to the documentation which clearly states that SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT "Specifies the HMAC salt. **This is only used if the password hash type is set to something other than plain text**. Defaults to None."

So I set a salt which theoretically could work with the plaintext hashing method:

```
app.config['SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT'] = b"xxx"
```

(I still leave the default for SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH which is 'plaintext'). The traceback disappears, but now the password validation is broken:

```
$ curl -i --data '{"password": "testpassword1", "email": "mail1"}' http://localhost:5000/login  -H "Content-Type: application/json"
HTTP/1.0 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 134
Set-Cookie: session=eyJfaWQiOnsiIGIiOiJNR0ZtTVRjd1lqVTRZMkppTjJFMk0yTm1NMll4TkRKbFltVXpNR0l5WW1FPSJ9fQ.CCu1kA.pNVcVQFzFV-tfK2KAPj2pCMivko; HttpOnly; Path=/
Server: Werkzeug/0.10.4 Python/2.7.3
Date: Wed, 06 May 2015 14:24:16 GMT

{
  "meta": {
    "code": 400
  },
  "response": {
    "errors": {
      "password": [
        "Invalid password"
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

That is, the password validation method does not properly account for this scenario, right?

So I set a hashing method different from plaintext:

```
app.config['SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH'] = "sha512_crypt"
```

Now the password validation works and I retrieve an auth token. But, really, this should work with the default settings (no hash method, no salt), or there should be an improved error message.

As a side note: As you can see above, the response always contains a `Set-Cookie: session=eyJfaWQiOnsiIGIi......` header. Why is that? Is that proper behavior? Does it come from the Flask-Login extension? Its documentation states that it only sets a cookie when using `remember=True` upon executing the login routine. How can I make the minimal app shown above make _not_ set a cookie upon each request? Should I open another issue for that?

issue_title: rewrite tests?
Equality filter should use '==' instead of '='
issue_body:
At least, that is how several other jsonpath implementations implement this.

E.g. the expression `$.[?(@.foo='bar')]` fails to parse in jayway's JsonPath implementation.  In the implementation [here](http://www.jsonquerytool.com/), `=` is used for assignment to the attribute and `==` is used to compare for equality.

issue_title: Split individual param from group of params

Splitting individual/group management would increase coherence and make the interface clearer and easier to use.

`SSMParameter` would only handle individual parameters, while `SSMParameterGroup` would consistently handle caching/refreshing of multiple parameters.

issue_title: Confusing results of **to_bytestring** method
issue_body:
Cacheback uses (this code)[https://github.com/codeinthehole/django-cacheback/blob/master/cacheback/base.py#L22-L33] for the *batteries included* version of FunctionJob.

Because it calls **str** on the args it can lead to wrong results if the value of that call is same for two **different** objects.

An example of such case are Django models instances which can, for two different objects, have same **str** return value.

Therefore I suggest we either fix this (preferable solution) or expand the docs to document this behaviour.

I'm happy to contribute code on this via a PR - just wanted to touch base first and discuss the direction.

issue_title: Support error filtering by level
issue_body:
In `docutils`, the reporters allow for filtering of errors via `report_level`. While we are circumventing this part of their system, we can reproduce this behavior.

``` python
errors = [error for error in errors if error.level <= self.report_level]
```

In `docutils`, these levels are 1 (most detailed) to 5 (no details) and can be accessed via the CLI with `--report, -r <level>`

http://repo.or.cz/w/docutils.git/blob/422cede485668203abc01c76ca317578ff634b30:/docutils/docutils/frontend.py#l429

issue_title: Create Script to find Top N keys by Used Memory
issue_body:
A very frequent use case is finding top N keys by memory usage. For small databases, this is easily achieved by taking the csv file and sorting out.

But for databases with more than 64K keys, the csv approach fails because excel cannot handle that many rows. 

The solution is to have a script that does a linear pass of all keys, maintain the top N keys by memory usage, and then finally print that out. Perhaps we could also maintain some more statistics as well.

See discussion on this over here - http://stackoverflow.com/questions/13673058/what-is-the-easiest-way-to-find-the-biggest-objects-in-redis/13681596#comment18794833_13681596

issue_title: Configuration options for ApprovalTests
issue_body:
It would be nice if it was possible to configure the behaviour of `ApprovalTests`.

One concrete use case I have is that I'd like to be able to store the `*.approved.txt` files in a subdirectory (say, `approved_files/`) rather than having them alongside the test files, which looks very cluttered and makes it hard to see what's going on. I presume there are other things for which configuration options would also be nice.

Does the original `ApprovalTests` (i.e., the non-Python version) have a way of specifying configuration options? I suspect it should be easy to point `ApprovalTests` to a custom configuration file (and/or make it read a configuration file with a standard name by default). I'm happy to have a shot at implementing this but thought I'd ask whether there is already anything that I missed because it's not documented, and also whether you have any ideas how it should ideally behave.

Many thanks!

class ClsMetric(mx.metric.EvalMetric):
    def __init__(self, output_names=None):

        self.num_inst[1] += (label > 0).sum()
        
    def get(self):

I'm not sure if I didn't compile the MXNet code correctly. But before compilation, the only change I made in the `config.mk` is to enable CUDA.

Anyone could comment on that? Is there anything I need to set properly for MXNet in order to reduce the memory usage?

issue_title: For gluon, how to define NET (MODEL) and LOSS for multi_label?
issue_body:
This maybe a complex question about applicaiton of mxnet.  And I really got stuck for a loooog time. I hope someone could help me, thanks~

**target:**
I want to transfer[ the multi_label net](https://github.com/xlvector/learning-dl/blob/master/mxnet/ocr/cnn_ocr.py) to gluon version. The core idea is to learn to predict 4 labels in a net. l And I have 2 problems with mxnet 0.12 on win10.

First, accordingly, for gluon I wrote with hybridize like:
```
class Net(gluon.HybridBlock):
    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
       super(Net, self).__init__(**kwargs)

        with self.name_scope():
            self.cov1 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(channels=32, kernel_size=(5, 5))
            self.cov2 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(channels=32, kernel_size=(5, 5))
            self.cov3 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(channels=32, kernel_size=(3, 3))

            self.max_pool = gluon.nn.MaxPool2D(pool_size=(2, 2), strides=(1, 1))
            self.avg_pool = gluon.nn.AvgPool2D(pool_size=(2, 2), strides=(1, 1))

            self.flatten = gluon.nn.Flatten()
            self.dense_256 = gluon.nn.Dense(256)
            self.dense_10 = gluon.nn.Dense(10)

    def hybrid_forward(self, F, x):
        x = F.relu(self.max_pool(self.cov1(x)))
        x = F.relu(self.max_pool(self.cov2(x)))
        for _ in range(2):
            x = F.relu(self.avg_pool(self.cov3(x)))

        x = self.flatten(x)
        x = F.relu(self.dense_256(x))
        x1 = self.dense_10(x)  # for 10 nums
        x2 = self.dense_10(x)
        x3 = self.dense_10(x)
        x4 = self.dense_10(x)
        out1 = F.concat(*[x1, x2, x3, x4], dim=0)
        return out1

        # return x1
        # maybe no need to use the following  3 statements in gluon
        # label = mx.symbol.transpose(data = label)
        # label = mx.symbol.Reshape(data = label, target_shape = (0,))
        # return mx.symbol.SoftmaxOutput(data=fc2, label=label, name="softmax")
```

About the net:
**Q1; As I didn't find any example to modify label when buiding net in hybrid class,  I guess it is correct to just end with `out1 = F.concat(*[x1, x2, x3, x4], dim=0)`?**

Then, when defining the 'trainer' (gluon version), the core code is like:
```
for i, batch in enumerate(train_iter):  # data shape = BYXC, label shape = (batchsize,)
    # get batch info and store data label on GPU
    data, label, batch_size = get_batch(batch, ctx)
    with autograd.record():
        output = net(data)  # data shape must be BCYX, output shape = (batchsize*Label_NUM,SOFTMAX_NUM)
        label = label.T.reshape((-1,)) # before: label shape = (batchsize*Label_NUM,)
        L = losser(output, label) 
    L.backward()
    trainer.step(batch_size)

def get_batch(batch, ctx):
    """return data and label on ctx"""
    '''the batch shape should be BCYX'''
    if isinstance(batch, list):  # for gluon.data.DataLoader
        data, label = batch
    else:  # for (subclass) of mx.io.DataBatch
        data, label = batch.data[0], batch.label[0]

    return (data.as_in_context(ctx), label.as_in_context(ctx), data.shape[0])  # data.shape[0] = batch_size
    # if no use try_all_gpu(),TypeError: object of type 'Context' has no len()
    # return (gluon.utils.split_and_load(data, ctx),
    #         gluon.utils.split_and_load(label, ctx),
    #         data.shape[0])
```
And here with the losser(loss function):
**Q2: As it would not be treated as correct unless all 4 labels in 1 example are matched,then is `  label = label.T.reshape((-1,))` essential and correct  here to calculate  loss?**  by the way, even for the referred old version, I still didn't understand why the label need to transpose and flat to a long vector.

issue_title: Change Network parameters in Gluon API
issue_body:
For the example, let say I want to have a dropout probability that change over iteration.
In NDArray API I can do it like that (from gluon tutoriel): 
```python
def net(X, drop_prob=0.0):
    h1_linear = nd.dot(X, W1) + b1
    h1 = relu(h1_linear)
    h1 = dropout(h1, drop_prob)
    h2_linear = nd.dot(h1, W2) + b2
    h2 = relu(h2_linear)
    h2 = dropout(h2, drop_prob)
    yhat_linear = nd.dot(h2, W3) + b3
    return yhat_linear
```
and in the training loop I can do that : 
```python
for e in range(epochs):
    for i, (data, label) in enumerate(train_data):
        data = data.as_in_context(ctx).reshape((-1,784))
        label = label.as_in_context(ctx)
        label_one_hot = nd.one_hot(label, 10)
        with autograd.record():
            output = net(data, drop_prob=.5**e)    # <-- here I can change dropout probability
            loss = softmax_cross_entropy(output, label_one_hot)
        loss.backward()
        SGD(params, learning_rate)
```

How can we do that with the gluon interface ? : 
```python
net = gluon.nn.Sequential()
with net.name_scope():
    net.add(gluon.nn.Dense(num_hidden, activation="relu"))
    net.add(gluon.nn.Dropout(.5))
    net.add(gluon.nn.Dense(num_hidden, activation="relu"))
    net.add(gluon.nn.Dropout(.5))
    net.add(gluon.nn.Dense(num_outputs))
```
In training loop: 
```python
for e in range(epochs):
    for i, (data, label) in enumerate(train_data):
        data = data.as_in_context(ctx).reshape((-1, 784))
        label = label.as_in_context(ctx)
        with autograd.record():
            output = net(data)                #  <-- how can I change dropout factor
            loss = softmax_cross_entropy(output, label)
            loss.backward()
        trainer.step(data.shape[0])
```

* DataLoader is implemented based on DataIter.
* We pad input images to a fixed size, so it doesn't change across GPU.

### If you have any thoughts on debugging, please comment and I'll report the results.

## What have you tried to solve it?

1. update nvidia driver to 384, cuda to 9.0 and cudnn to 7.1.2
2. gradually reducing the building blocks of the architecture, and found that the time taken for the error to show gradually increases. So it is unlikely that the behavior is triggered by one operator mis-usage.
3. changing DataLoader to single-threaded version.
4. removing callbacks(like speedometer) and metrics.
5. building mxnet with flag DMSHADOW_USE_PASCAL turned on, as instructed by this issue: https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/issues/6592, which didn't work.
6. turning python gc on and off in each epoch, as instructed by this issue: https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/issues/7710 and didn't work.

issue_title: Support for `nullable`
issue_body:
Hi, 

I know that bravado uses `x-nullable` to designate any property as something that can have `null` value. The [official documentation](http://bravado-core.readthedocs.io/en/latest/models.html#allowing-null-values-for-properties) suggests that maintainers of bravado were waiting for the specification update. (https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/pull/894/). Now that [`nullable` is a part of the specification](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/3.0.1.md), would it be possible for bravado to support `nullable`? 

If there is support for it, I could create a PR for review.
