issue_title: fix command line plotting programs for Windows
issue_body:
Here’s more documentation about interactive mode: https://matplotlib.org/faq/usage_faq.html#what-is-interactive-mode

Below is the minimal code I used to debug pmagplotlib.  There are command line switches for -t (use TKagg), -i (use plt.ion), and -p (print an input() call at the end of the plotting).

Basically, it only works to have an interactive plot AND access to the command line (for save/quit/continue):  on OSX, with TKinter backend, with plt.ion, and with an “input()” call to prevent the program from just instantly quitting.  

On Windows, with tkinter plt.ion and input(), the plot isn’t actually filled in and is “not responding”.  If I take out plt.ion, it works, but is “blocking”.  As far as I can tell from the docs, this is exactly how matplotlib is supposed to work.


    import matplotlib
    import sys
    if "-t" in sys.argv:
        matplotlib.use("TKAgg")
    from matplotlib import pyplot as plt

    if "-i" in sys.argv:
        plt.ion()
        print('not blocking')
    else:
        print('blocking')

    plt.figure(1)
    plt.plot([1,2,3])
    plt.draw()
    plt.show()
    #plt.ioff()

issue_title: Question about the "Advanced readers: skipgram versus cbow" part of the tutorial.
issue_body: Hello and thank you for this awesome library! Looking over the cool new tutorial and noticed something that made me pause for a bit. When discussing the difference between CBOW and skipgram, the tutorial states: > The skipgram model learns to predict a target word thanks to a nearby word. On the other hand, the cbow model predicts the target word according to its context. The context is represented as a bag of the words contained in a fixed size window around the target word. ![cbo_vs_skipgram](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/13853804/25653118/cb7aa9d4-2feb-11e7-8924-e5e03437bf42.png) Maybe I am misunderstanding, but to me this seems a little bit misleading. I totally agree with the description of CBOW, but my understanding of skipgram was that it tries to predict the context words around the same input word, as illustrated in the paper by Mikolov et al. (Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space): Therefore, in the case of the leather jacket the input would always be "fine", but we are trying to predict "selling", "these", etc. All the best, Arnor

issue_title: wordNgrams in unsupervised mode (cbow and skipgram)
issue_body:
Hi, quick question.

I see that word n-grams (i.e., `-wordNgrams`) are only used in supervised mode, and not in cbow nor skipgram. 

Is there a reason for this?

The documentation is not clear on this point, but the code calls `addWordNgrams()` only [here](https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/blob/3e64bf0f5b916532b34be6706c161d7d0a4957a4/src/dictionary.cc#L376) inside `Dictionary::getLine()` used by supervised training and not [here](https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/blob/3e64bf0f5b916532b34be6706c161d7d0a4957a4/src/dictionary.cc#L349) in the equivalent `Dictionary::getLine()` used by unsupervised methods.

Thanks.

issue_title: Switch cache to LRU?
issue_body: Python 3 provides an implementation of an least-recently-used cache (https://docs.python.org/3/library/functools.html#functools.lru_cache) (which is written in C since 3.5, so it'll be hard to beat its performance).  Using it for caching would avoid having to deal with multithreading, and the availability of `cache_info()` would let the user have a better idea of how to tune the cache parameter (in this case, the cache size, rather than the cache lifetime; I feel that the latter is difficult to set in an informed manner).

1,4
foo,bar
```

## All quoting, option 1
```bash
> echo -e "a,b\n4,2\n1,4" | csvsort -u 1
a,b
1,4
4,2
```
expected:

```bash
"a","b"
"1","4"
"4","2"
```

## Quote non-numeric, option 2
```bash
> echo -e "a,b\n4,2\nfoo,bar" | csvsort -u 2
could not convert string to float: 'a'
```

expected
```bash
"a","b"
4,2
"foo","bar"
```

## Quote None, option 3
```bash
echo -e "a,b\n4,2\n\"foo\",\"bar\"" | csvsort -u 3
a,b
"""foo""","""bar"""
4,2
```

expected
```bash
a,b
4,2
foo,bar
```

Options 1, 2, 3 do not seem to be working.

## Issue description
My goal is to create a wrapper to the python default `file` type.
For that I created a C++ class (with no base class) and I want to expose it as a subclass of `file`. Which I cannot do as `file` isn't itself exported by pybind11.

issue_title: Random appveyor x86 build failures
issue_body: I'm seeing occassional random build failures on the appveyor x86 builds, always during the linking stage.  I have a feeling we're running into memory limitations, perhaps combined with the addition of the /m flag for parallel building and the new multi-CPU abilities.  (I haven't yet seen a failure for exactly the same builds on https://ci.appveyor.com/project/jagerman/pybind11).  If this persists, perhaps we should turn off the /m flag? (I'm assuming--though may be wrong--that it's default to 2 cores for the main project builds and 1 for my open-source-free account)

issue_title: Detect and handle ManagedDomain directive
issue_body: The [mod_md Apache module](https://github.com/icing/mod_md/) will add native ACME support to Apache. I think Certbot should probably look for the main directive `ManagedDomain` and not try to autoconfigure Apache when that's present. Otherwise I think the results are likely to get complicated and hard to support.

issue_title: Certbot fails when using an invalid `/etc/hosts` configuration and does not print a specific diagnostic error.
issue_body:
I set up a server with an incorrect ip address associated with the host name of the server in its `/etc/hosts` file, causing `certbot-auto` to fail when attempting to create a certificate with the `--apache` method. It would be nice if certbot detected this specific issue and printed a helpful message specific to the error.

```
2016-12-07 16:55:49,372:DEBUG:certbot.reporter:Reporting to user: The following errors were reported by the server:

Domain: my.domain
Type:   connection
Detail: Failed to connect to 111.111.111.111:443 for TLS-SNI-01 challenge

To fix these errors, please make sure that your domain name was entered correctly and the DNS A record(s) for that domain contain(s) the right IP address. Additionally, please check that your computer has a publicly routable IP address and that no firewalls are preventing the server from communicating with the client. If you're using the webroot plugin, you should also verify that you are serving files from the webroot path you provided.
```

I set up my server using xen and a custom fai system, but using an incorrect ip address. I corrected the host's dns entry and fixed /etc/network/interfaces and the /etc/xen/my.domain.cfg file, but I did not change `/etc/hosts` until I debugged the source of the issue. To test this issue, you could try setting up Trisquel 7 or any GNU/Linux system and adding (or changing) an entry in /etc/hosts, inserting the incorrect ip address for the FQDN and PQDN of the host. As far as I know, the incorrect ip address I used was not in use by any other host on the internet at the time of testing.

As an aside, apache2 was serving http over the https port when incorrectly configured like this. You can see this by running:

    curl http://my.domain:443/

If you would like more details, see the following thread:

* https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/certbot-auto-unable-to-complete-tls-sni-01-challenge-on-ubuntu-14-04/23657

Thank you.

issue_title: Failures in storage.py
issue_body:
Usage: any invocation of `certbot`

Installation: `sudo pacman -S certbot` => `/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/certbot`

Error: An unexpected error occurred: ValueError: max() args is an empty sequence

Traceback: storage.py, line 682, in latest_common_version

Code:
```python
    def latest_common_version(self):
        """Newest version for which all items are available?

        :returns: the newest available version for which all members
            (``cert, ``privkey``, ``chain``, and ``fullchain``) exist
        :rtype: int

        """
        # TODO: this can raise CertStorageError if there is no version overlap
        #       (it should probably return None instead)
        # TODO: this can raise a spurious AttributeError if the current
        #       link for any kind is missing (it should probably return None)
        versions = [self.available_versions(x) for x in ALL_FOUR]
        return max(n for n in versions[0] if all(n in v for v in versions[1:]))
```

Fix:
```python
        #return max(n for n in versions[0] if all(n in v for v in versions[1:]))
        try:
                return max(n for n in versions[0] if all(n in v for v in versions[1:]))
        except:
                return 1
```

issue_title: Renew asking for domains even though config file is correct.
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):
Debian Wheezy

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
certbot-auto

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
/usr/local/bin/certbot-auto && /usr/local/bin/certbot-auto renew
Saving debug log to /var/log/letsencrypt/letsencrypt.log
No names were found in your configuration files. Please enter in your domain
name(s) (comma and/or space separated)  (Enter 'c' to cancel):

issue_title: Properly handle modified directory URLs in ACME
issue_body: Earlier it was suggested just making these URLs optional parameters to the affected methods in `ClientBase` and having the derived classes provide them to the base class methods. Another possible approach is to have the `Directory` class try either naming convention (ACMEv1 version or camel case) and only fail if neither directory field exists. If both exist, we can either favor ACMEv1 or ACMEv2 or raise an error.

issue_title: Domain selected from list behaves differently from domain specified by -d
issue_body:
[letsencrypt.txt](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/files/220468/letsencrypt.txt)

The attached log resulted from running ./letsencrypt-auto, selecting only one domain, namely www.tamatzner.de, from the offered list of domains. The error, as discussed in https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/name-does-not-end-in-a-public-suffix-on-eu-and-de-domain/14270, probably resulted from an attempt to certify another domain, namely horde.webmail. This domain wasn't even offered in the list of selectable domains.

Anyway, when running ./letsencrypt-auto -d www.tamatzner.de, the error disappeared, so the both don't appear to be exactly equivalent.

I'd be ready to share my Apache configuration. But this is a Plesk-managed server that has quite a lot of auto-generated config files. Also, by fiddling around with SSL options, they probably got modified since the time the error occurred.

issue_title: Replace some python_ta._check code with appropriate config options
issue_body:
`python_ta._check` takes several arguments that can (and should) be put into the `pylintrc` file instead, or alternatively run by command line (inside Python):
- [ ] reporter: use `--output-format=<reporter module>`.  (Might need to use `--init-hook=<Python code>` to add directory to search for reporter module to PYTHONPATH.)
- [ ] level: currently sent to reporter's `print_messages`, but as may be mentioned on the reporters page, reporters should actually override `BaseReporter.display_messages`. Also, level is currently used to indicate "display code errors, display style errors, or all", but this should instead be given using `--disable=<category>` in `pylintrc`, where `<category>` is one of "C, W, E, F, R, standing for Convention, Warning, Error, Fatal and Refactoring" ([Pylint docs](https://pylint.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference_guide/custom_checkers.html)).
- [ ] local_config_file: doesn't need to be indicated in `pylintrc`, but can be indicated by the command line from within Python using `--rcfile=<config file>`. (See [invoking pylint programmatically](http://stackoverflow.com/a/2062665) on Stack Overflow.)
- [ ] Figure out what is going on with the quirk below:

It seems that running this:

    pylint --rcfile=<current pylintrc> --<option-1>=<value-1> --<option-2>=<value-2> --generate-rcfile

applies `<option-1>` and `<option-2>` in the generated config file, but does not apply the options given in the `<current pylintrc>` file. This is very strange and bears further investigation.

issue_title: Question regarding Pyarmor licensing mechanism
issue_body:
Hello,

We just bought a license of pyarmor for a commercial project for which I am the main developer.

I installed pyarmor into a conda environment using pip.

That installed a license file with the python package here:

`/home/awagner/miniconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pyarmor/license.lic`

Is that the file that we're supposed to replace with our purchased license?

Is the uniqueness of this file what prevents our customers from downloading pyarmor and generating licenses for our product themselves? Or would that have been impossible even without a paid pyarmor license?

I am asking because there is a chance pip will overwrite that file in the future with a trial license, and I need to make sure we don't accidentally ship unprotected code.

Thanks,
Andrew

issue_title: Create `smoketest` command for CLI
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

We should have a standardized way to minimally "self test" a raiden build/installation. That would allow to
- minimize debugging search space/help pointing out installation environment problems
- safe guard against regressions from refactoring (CLI is not extensively tested in CI)
- fail faster in CI
- have a reasonable testing path for binary bundles (the `AppImage`) in CI

## Solution

The idea is to have a `raiden smoketest` command in CLI that will test the general sanity of the installation. In detail it would

    - start `geth` in a `tempdir` on a random free port
    - use a known `account`
    - use a `smoketest_genesis` that has the raiden contracts deployed and some channels
        already opened (genesis should be created on `python setup.py install` )
    - connect to the contracts
    - test `EndpointRegistry` (register our own endpoint)
    - test channel collection from Registry
    - test Transport available
        - try to setup transport (open a port)
        - try to setup protocol on top of transport
        - try to send a ping to our own port (?) (could be a channel partner with the same
            endpoint pre-registered in `smoketest_genesis` )
    - shut down

The smoketest can later be expanded to include other features like WAL, snapshotting, recovery.

issue_title: sos execute --fetch-result
issue_body:
When a job is executed on the remote server, the results would be available on the remote host, with status `result-ready`. Generally speaking, if we are running in `--no-wait` mode, we will need to run `sos run -q` at least twice, the first time get the tasks submitted, and the second time to collect the results (if the tasks are ready), and continue the workflow if needed.

```
sos run script -q remote
sos run script -q remote
```

For a particular task, we can run

```
sos execute task_id -q remote
```

and if the task has already been done, this command would simply sync the result to the local host. Then, it might make sense to add an option to this command

```
sos execute task_id -q remote --fetch-result
```

so that it does not do anything if the result is not ready (namely do not actually execute the task). We might be able to expand the syntax to something like

```
sos execute -q remote --fetch-result --all
```
to check and retrieve results for all remote tasks.

That said, a dedicated `fetch` command might be more intuitive.

```
sos fetch task_id -q remote
sos fetch --all -q remote
```

issue_title: Standardize Method Names
issue_body:
We have several endpoints that are prefixed with `list_` while others are prefixed with `get_`. For consistency's sake, we need to standardize which form to use. After internal discussion, we have decided that `get_` seems the most appropriate.

Change all method names that start with `list_` to start with `get_`.

Because some folks may already be using the `list_` methods, we can't just rename the methods - we'll need deprecate them and direct users to switch to the new `get_` methods. Most likely move the code to the `get_` version and have the `list_` version warn with a `DeprecationWarning` and call the `get_` version.

issue_title: Potential name collision between language host and language host executor
issue_body:
`pulumi-langhost-nodejs-exec` and `pulumi-langhost-nodejs` share the same prefix, which means that we will fail in unexpected ways if a customer lists `nodejs-exec` as their runtime. We should discuss the implications of this.

See this thread https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/pull/901#discussion_r167102380 for details.

issue_title: reconnecting cluster slave nodes to the head node fails
issue_body:
Steps to reproduce:
Start head node: 
`scripts/start_ray.sh --num-cpus 100 --num-gpus 0 --num-workers 100 --head`
Login to the second (slave) node and start ray, pointing to the head node:
`./scripts/start_ray.sh --redis-address <headnode_ip:redis_port>`
Then stop Ray on the slave node : 
`./scripts/stop_ray.sh `

And now try to start Ray on the slave node again: 
```
./scripts/start_ray.sh --redis-address <headnode_ip:redis_port>
Waiting for redis server at <headnode_ip:redis_port> to respond...
Using IP address ####### for this node.
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/data/atumanov/ray/scripts/start_ray.py", line 109, in <module>
    check_no_existing_redis_clients(node_ip_address, args.redis_address)
  File "/data/atumanov/ray/scripts/start_ray.py", line 34, in check_no_existing_redis_clients
    raise Exception("This Redis instance is already connected to clients with this IP address.")
Exception: This Redis instance is already connected to clients with this IP address.
```

Takeaway: starting and stopping Ray on slave nodes is not idempotent and it should be.

issue_title: [xray] Terminated actors with queued tasks crashes raylet
issue_body:
### Describe the problem
Raylet expects actor tasks to be scheduled in order of submission. This is enforced with the actor task counter field in the task spec. However, the `__ray_terminate__` task, submitted when the original actor handle goes out of scope, is scheduled to execute as soon as possible. This means that the terminate task will get scheduled before any other tasks that are queued for the same actor. Raylet then crashes because the terminate task has a higher task counter than expected.

### Source code / logs
To reproduce:
```python
import ray
import time
ray.init(use_raylet=True)

@ray.remote(num_cpus=0)
class Actor(object):
     def __init__(self):
         return
     def foo(self):
         time.sleep(1)
         return

a = Actor.remote()
# Submit a bunch of tasks.
for _ in range(1000):
  a.foo.remote()
# Delete the actor, which triggers the `__ray_terminate__` task.
del a
```

issue_title: Simplify cancel_shielded_checkpoint ?
issue_body:
I'm not sure what is the need for a mix of `yield` and `asyncfunction` decorator here:

https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/a351a2d148032dccf2b06636efbf4399bc0e24a4/trio/_core/_traps.py#L13-L45

From what I understand, it is because an awaited object must be of a type with an `__await__` method.
But in such a case there could be a much simpler wait to do this:

```python
class CancelShieldedCheckpointType:
    def __await__(self):
        yield self
CancelShieldedCheckpoint = CancelShieldedCheckpoint()
del CancelShieldedCheckpointType

async def cancel_shielded_checkpoint():
    return await CancelShieldedCheckpoint
```

This way `CancelShieldedCheckpointType` is just a helper class (hence destroyed as soon at it is no longer needed to avoid) and `CancelShieldedCheckpoint` is still a singleton, this time compatible with `await` syntax.

issue_title: S3 GET Response header Content-Type should be 'binary/octet-stream' not 'text/html'
issue_body:
If you make a GET request to URL for a file that is publicly readable, the content-type is being set to `text/html`, but when you do the same for the real S3, the content-type is set to `binary/octet-stream`.  This allows the browser to know to just download it as a file, instead of trying to display it.

## To Reproduce:

```python
import boto3
import os
import requests
from botocore.config import Config
from StringIO import StringIO

host = 'localhost'
port = 4572
s3 = boto3.resource('s3',
 config=Config(s3={'addressing_style': 'path'}),
 endpoint_url="http://{}:{}".format(host, port)
)

s3.create_bucket(Bucket='test-bucket')
bucket = s3.Bucket('test-bucket')

key = 'blah.txt'
file_obj = StringIO('hello world')
bucket.upload_fileobj(file_obj, key, ExtraArgs={"ACL": 'public-read'})
url = s3.meta.client.generate_presigned_url(
    'get_object', ExpiresIn=0, Params={'Bucket': bucket.name, 'Key': key}
)
url = url.split('?')[0]

response = requests.get(url)
print response.headers['content-type']
```

When running this against AWS S3, the content-type for txt, pdf, csv and html filetype extensions is `binary/octet-stream`.    When running the same thing but connected to localstack, I always get `text/html; charset=utf-8`.

issue_title: pip 10.0.0 breaks localstack build
issue_body:
`make docker-build` seems to be failing both locally and in the travis builds.

At least one dependency (`warrant-ext`) uses parts of the `pip` package as an API (e.g. it calls `from pip.req import parse_requirements`). Version 10.0.0 of `pip` makes it very clear that `pip`'s modules are not to be used by anyone else by putting everything into a new `_internal` package. 

It looks like localstack does a `pip install --upgrade pip` before installing anything, so it always uses `pip==10.0.0`, and then tries to build requirements (like `warrent-ext`) that try to use what's been moved to the internal package. localstack will need to do either or both of these steps:

1. Pin pip to version `9.0.3`
2. Remove or update dependencies that use this internal API incorrectly.

issue_title: Inconsistent results when iterating and indexing Tree
issue_body:
When iterating over the entries in a Tree, it seems like some entries appear in the iteration, but can't be retrieved by using the entry name as an index to the Tree object. I noticed this when trying to read the Git repository for the Linux kernel. There are some tree entries in 'include/linux' which show the problem. The tree entries / directories named 'can', 'hdlc', 'i2c', 'input', 'netfilter_bridge', 'rtc' and 'wimax' all show up in the list of entries when iterating over the tree for the directory 'include/linux', but can't be retrieved by, e.g.,  tree['can'] - this throws a KeyError.

I uploaded a small gist which shows up the problem: https://gist.github.com/3bcc088234d2de488258

One common feature to all of these is that there are also files which have the directory name as a prefix in the tree, e.g. 'can.h', 'hdlc.h'. Other entries, including directories like 'dvb', can be retrieved from the Tree object by name.

As far as I can see, Tree objects are supposed to be index-able by the names of entries in the Tree, as well as by a numeric index. Assuming this is correct, it looks like a bug in either pygit2 or libgit2.

I'm using the latest revisions of pygit2 (28b1debfa7c13fdbcdee0d237c69d62746f993de) and libgit2 (77bb37eb47647180c0ed30517f6c6e4c219d131a). The operating system is Ubuntu 10.04.3, and the Python / GCC versions are:

diff --git a/MANIFEST.in b/MANIFEST.in

index 0000000..bb37a27
--- /dev/null
+++ b/MANIFEST.in
@@ -0,0 +1 @@
+include *.rst
 ```

issue_title: slow crc32
issue_body:
Please, use fast crc32 from zlib. It is about 18 times faster:

import zlib
import couchbase.crc32 as couch_slow_crc
import time

def Func1(amax=100000):
   t1=time.clock()
   for i in range(amax):
      x=couch_slow_crc.crc32_hash(`i`)
   t2=time.clock()
   return t2-t1
   
def Func2(amax=100000):
   t1=time.clock()
   for i in range(amax):
      x=(zlib.crc32(`i`)>>16)&0x7fff
   t2=time.clock()
   return t2-t1


def Func3(amax=100000):
   for i in range(amax):
      v1=(zlib.crc32(`i`)>>16)&0x7fff
      v2=couch_slow_crc.crc32_hash(`i`)
      if v1!=v2:
         print 'conflict %d %x %x'%(i,v1,v2)
   t2=time.clock()

t1=Func1()
t2=Func2()
print 'time couch',t1
print 'time zlib',t2
print 'speed',t1/t2
Func3()

#######################################
time couch 1.83402449707
time zlib 0.0990188503142
speed 18.5219732531

issue_title: Splinter leaves "tests" folder in site-packages
issue_body:
After installing splinter, it puts "tests" in /path/to/env/lib/python-{VERSION}/site-packages.

I just found it because I need to import a tests module, and it was in the wrong path. Look:


    >>> import tests
    Traceback (most recent call last):
    File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
    File "/private/tmp/splinter-bug/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tests/__init__.py", line 2, in <module>
      from fake_webapp import start_server, stop_server
    File "/private/tmp/splinter-bug/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tests/fake_webapp.py", line 1, in <module>
      from flask import Flask
    ImportError: No module named flask


The fix is super easy, if I am not wrong, it just needs to exclude "tests" folder in setup.py. Maybe the change is the following:

    --- setup.py	2011-11-08 18:16:09.000000000 -0200
    +++ setup.py.fixed	2011-11-08 18:16:40.000000000 -0200
    @@ -9,9 +9,8 @@
           long_description=README,
           author='CobraTeam',
           author_email='andrewsmedina@gmail.com',
    -      packages=find_packages(),
    +     packages=find_packages(exclude=["tests"]),
           include_package_data=True,

issue_title: Tests break when run under windows
issue_body:
Norl...@gmail.com, 2010-12-19T00:27:58.000Z 
When doing run_tests.py in rdflib-3.0.0 under windows using python 2.5 I got 3 failures due to URLError
 and 2 doctest failures (one for Graph.parse and the other for util.make_date_time).

The URLError problems I traced back to when absolute_location is created on line 152 of parser.py it has a raw windows file path (in my case 'C:\working\rdflib-3.0.0\test\rdfa\w3c_rdfa_testsuite\rdfa-xhtml1-test-manifest.rdf' that gets passed on to URIRef() which then to urljoin() where the drive letter in front of the colon is mistaken for the url type (I don't know if this has been fixed in later python versions - I'm stuck using 2.5 for reasons beyond my control)... 

To fix this in parser.py between the lines
---------------------------------------------------------------------
150    if location is not None:
151        base = urljoin("file:", "%s/" % pathname2url(os.getcwd()))
---------------------------------------------------------------------
I inserted the following two lines
---------------------------------------------------------------------
150    if location is not None:
...        if os.path.exists(location):
...            location = pathname2url(location)
151        base = urljoin("file:", "%s/" % pathname2url(os.getcwd()))
---------------------------------------------------------------------
this also fixed the Graph.parse() doctest failure.

As for parse_date_time the failure occurs in the doctest:
"""
>>> parse_date_time('1970-01-01T00:00:00Z') - 0.0
    0.0
"""
due to an underflow in mktime()... while looking at this I tried my own test:
parse_date_time(date_time(0)) which failed for the same reason! but

"""
>> parse_date_time(date_time(36000))
   36000
""" passes
and
"""
>> parse_date_time(date_time(35999))
   35999
""" fails with the underflow problem also

 I noticed elsewhere on the issues list someone else has suggested adopting some code from a pre existing iso8601 implementation, here here!

issue_title: Timestamp parsing is one month off
issue_body:
---
    xmas: 2011-12-24
    ...

results in

    {
         xmas: Mon, 23 Jan 2012 23:00:00 GMT
    }

Confirmed this on the [website][1]

[1]: http://nodeca.github.com/js-yaml/#yaml=LS0tCnhtYXM6IDIwMTEtMTItMjQKLi4u

issue_title: Message without author or to goes /dev/null
issue_body: If by mistake one creates a Message without "author" or "to", marrow.mailer 4.0 says it sends the message, actually does not try to send it (after authenticating), and does not raise an exception. I don't think this behaviour is good; I'd prefer it to throw an exception.
